scenario: A team's process is critical and needs high observability. They want their BPMN modelling choices to support good Operate / Optimize visibility -- clear instance state, meaningful metrics.
question: How can BPMN modelling improve process observability?

options.text: Modelling best practices for observability: 1. , "Validate Customer Data" not "Task1") -- readable in Operate. 2. Granular activities: split monolithic logic into distinct tasks -- Operate shows each separately; track per-step latency. 3. Explicit error handling: Error Boundary Events with specific errorCodes -- Operate shows which error type fired. 4. Set business-meaningful variables: at key points, set variables for filtering / search (customer ID, order ID, status). 5. " 6. Subprocess for grouping: visual structure; subprocess instances tracked separately in Operate. 7. Comments / documentation: BPMN annotations explain non-obvious logic. These choices make the process "self-documenting" in Operate, enable quick troubleshooting, and feed meaningful data to Optimize for analytics.

BPMN modelling choices have no meaningful impact on how a process is observed in Operate or Optimize -- this misrepresents the strong relationship between design decisions and operational visibility. Every element name, variable set, error code, and activity boundary directly shapes what Operate and Optimize show to operators. Poorly named elements ("Task1"), monolithic tasks, and missing business-meaningful variables create blind spots that make troubleshooting time-consuming and audit trails incomplete. Good modelling is a concrete investment in long-term operational productivity.

Adding many None Intermediate Events as milestones is the primary way to improve process observability -- while None Intermediate Events as named milestones are one effective observability technique, they are only one element of a comprehensive strategy. Meaningful task and event names, granular activity decomposition, explicit error codes on Error Boundary Events, business-meaningful variables set at key points, and subprocess grouping all contribute equally or more significantly to operational visibility in Operate and Optimize.

Consolidating logic into large monolithic activities produces clearer process diagrams with fewer elements and better observability -- this is the opposite of best practice. Large monolithic activities hide per-step latency, merge multiple error sources into one opaque failure, and prevent granular retry configurations per step. Operate tracks activity-level metrics, so a monolithic task produces one opaque duration instead of per-step breakdowns. Granular activities enable root-cause analysis, targeted incident resolution, and step-level retry strategies with appropriate backoff.

a.text: Correct. BPMN modelling choices have major impact on operational observability: 1. Naming: clear element names appear in Operate's UI. "Validate Customer Address" reads better than "T-12" when debugging. 2. Granularity: split big monolithic tasks into smaller ones: Per-step latency visible in Operate. Per-step retry counts. Per-step error rates. Easier root cause analysis. 3. Explicit error handling: Error Boundary Events with specific codes: Operate shows "INVALID_DATA" vs "TIMEOUT" vs "RATE_LIMITED" distinctly. Filter incidents by error type. Targeted troubleshooting. 4. Business-meaningful variables: Set customerId, orderId, status at key points. Operate's variable filter finds instances by business attributes. Audit trail with business context. 5. Milestones: None Intermediate Events label significant transitions ("Order Confirmed", "Payment Received"). Operate's instance history shows passage; quick visual scan. 6. Subprocess: Visual structure: collapsible groupings. Subprocess instances tracked separately. Boundary events at subprocess level for scoped handling. 7.
